fields.description:
# S6 — Pluggable custom verifier

## Why
No fixed set of verifiers covers every organization's identity system (bespoke internal token
services, non-standard introspection like GitHub's, mTLS-header trust). A pluggable seam keeps the
capability open-ended — an operator drops in their own verifier without patching rebar — which is
core to the "generic, wide variety of environments" goal.

## What
Add a **custom-verifier** mode to `src/rebar/_mcp_auth.py`, selected by the `custom` token in
`mcp.auth_strategies`, with one flat config key it registers: `auth_custom_import` (a `module:factory`
import string resolving to a factory that returns an object implementing the official SDK
`mcp.server.auth.provider.TokenVerifier` protocol). The factory's returned verifier is placed in the
composite and treated like any built-in — crucially, **the composite's audience re-check still
applies**: a custom verifier that returns an `AccessToken` with `resource=None` or a wrong `resource`
is rejected by the composite (S2), so a buggy/hostile custom verifier cannot bypass RFC 8707. Loading
validates the resolved object satisfies the protocol and fails closed otherwise. Registers its key in
`MCP_ENV_VARS`/`server.json` + regenerates the reference docs.

## Approach & rationale
Expose the SAME `TokenVerifier` seam the built-ins use, loaded via a config-named import path (the
established Python import-string convention, e.g. how uvicorn/Starlette resolve app factories). This
keeps custom verifiers ecosystem-compatible and adds no new abstraction. The composite audience
re-check (S2) is what makes an untrusted-in-code-quality custom verifier still safe on the audience
axis.

## Scope
The custom-verifier loader + protocol validation only, on the S2 seam. Subset of the epic. Depends on
S2. Independent of S3/S4/S5.

## Security
Boundary-crossing AND it loads operator-configured code (arbitrary import executes at load):
- The import path is an **operator-supplied trusted config value** and is treated as a documented
  trust boundary. It is read ONLY from resolved config (`auth_custom_import`), **never** from a
  request or any untrusted source — enforced structurally (the value is consumed only at startup from
  the config object), not merely documented.
- Import resolution does **not** add the current working directory to `sys.path` (avoid cwd
  shadowing); resolution/validation failure, or a resolved object lacking `verify_token`, is a
  **fail-closed startup error** (never run with partial/no auth).
- The composite's mandatory audience re-check (S2) still applies to whatever the custom verifier
  returns — it cannot mint an accepted token for a wrong/absent audience.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] The `auth_custom_import` flat key is registered in `MCP_ENV_VARS`/`server.json`, appears in the
      regenerated reference docs, and `scripts/check_server_manifest.py` passes.
- [ ] Unit test (tier: unit) asserts a valid `module:factory` resolves to a working verifier whose
      accepted token (with `resource=auth_resource_server_url`) is honored by the composite
      (observable oracle: 200/accept) — and that resolution does not depend on cwd being on `sys.path`.
- [ ] Unit test asserts fail-closed startup for: an unresolvable import path, and a resolved object
      that does not implement `verify_token` (observable oracle: startup raises a clear message) —
      two negative controls.
- [ ] Unit test asserts a custom verifier returning an `AccessToken` with `resource=None` or a wrong
      `resource` is **rejected by the composite** (the custom seam cannot bypass the audience check).
- [ ] Integration test (httpx `ASGITransport`) asserts a token accepted by a configured custom
      verifier → 200 end-to-end through the transport.
- [ ] `docs/mcp-auth.md` (S7) documents that `auth_custom_import` is a trusted operator config value
      that executes code at load.
- [ ] `make lint && make typecheck && make test` pass.

## Compatibility
Additive, opt-in via the `custom` strategy. No change to other verifiers or stdio.
